Requirements for Plant Growth: All plants fundamentally require continuous access to both adequate sunlight and water to ensure vigorous structural growth and survival.
Without water, a plant will rapidly wither and die, even if it is placed in direct, abundant sunlight.
Photosynthesis (Food Production): Leaves uniquely function as the 'food factories' of the plant because their green pigment, chlorophyll, captures required sunlight.
Using sunlight, liquid water, and carbon dioxide forcefully absorbed from the air via tiny leaf pores (stomata), plants synthesize food continuously.
The direct products of this critical process (photosynthesis) are energy-rich simple glucose (often stored later as starch) and free oxygen released into the atmosphere.
Transport Systems within Plants: Plants possess specialized internal tube-like structures called the xylem, which act purely to transport water and dissolved soil minerals vertically up from the roots.
Simultaneously, a distinct downward and outward transport network, the phloem, diligently carries the food synthesized in the leaves to all other living parts of the plant.
Plant Respiration: Just like animals, every living part of a plant must constantly respire to unlock and harness usable energy required for cellular maintenance and growth.
During pure respiration, the plant chemically breaks down its internally stored glucose utilizing oxygen, thereby releasing energy, water, and carbon dioxide as byproducts.
